How to use this guide

Start with the Foundations section — it builds the "why" before the "what" and is required reading before your first practice-cockpit call. Then read sections 1–5 to build the technical mental model. Sections 6–9 cover what you'll sell alongside the compressor (dryers, filters, separators). Sections 10–13 are the sales-conversation layer. Come back to the glossary as needed.

2
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

Foundations · 0.1 What Is an Air Compressor?

Read this before you open the rest of the study guide. Read it before your first call in the practice cockpit. Nothing else in this training makes sense until the ideas here click.

An air compressor is a machine that squeezes outside air into a smaller space, raising its pressure, so the pressurized air can do work somewhere else in the building.

That's it. You pack a lot of air into a little space, pipe it where it needs to go, and let it push tools, valves, cylinders, and conveyors along the way.

You've probably heard compressed air called "the fourth utility." The first three — electricity, water, and natural gas — are obvious; they come into the building from a pipe or a wire. Compressed air is different. The factory has to make its own, usually in a dedicated room with one or more compressors running most of the day. But the plant depends on it the same way it depends on the power company. Shut off electricity and the plant stops. Shut off compressed air and the plant stops just as fast.

Nearly every manufacturing facility in North America runs on it. Food plants, automotive, pharma, metalworking, semiconductor, paint, plastics, textiles, breweries, woodshops — if something is being made, something on that floor is probably running on air.

One sentence to keep in your head

A compressor takes free outside air and turns it into a controlled, on-demand power source that drives the work on the factory floor.

3
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

Foundations · 0.2 Why It Matters to Customers

Three things your customer cares about, in order of how often they'll admit to them.

Uptime

When the compressor goes down, the line stops. No air means no tools, no cylinders, no conveyors, no paint, no packaging. For a plant running a 24/7 shift, one hour of downtime can cost tens of thousands of dollars in lost production, idle labor, and missed customer commitments. Your customer knows this in their bones. They don't always know the number — but they feel it every time an alarm goes off at 3 a.m.

Cost

Compressed air is usually the most expensive utility per unit of useful work delivered. Of every dollar of electricity fed into the compressor, only 10–15 cents comes out as useful work at the tool. The rest turns into heat, leaks, and losses in the distribution piping. That makes the compressor one of the largest line items on their power bill, typically behind only HVAC and primary production equipment.

The hidden cost problem

Here's the opening you need to understand. Most facility managers cannot tell you what their compressed air actually costs them. They know their electric bill. They do not know how much of that bill is compressed air, how much of that is wasted on leaks, or how much they'd save with the right equipment. The information is knowable — we can measure it — but nobody has ever sat down and showed it to them.

The wedge

This is why we get in the door. We know something they don't. Not because we're smarter than they are, but because we spend all day looking at compressed air and they spend all day running a factory. Our job on the call is not to sell them a compressor. Our job is to help them see a cost they've been paying invisibly for years, and then show them what to do about it.

4
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

Foundations · 0.3 How Compressed Air Gets to the Point of Use

Walk into any compressor room and you'll see the same flow, every time, in this order.

Compressor → Aftercooler → Dryer → Filters → Receiver Tank → Piping → Point of Use

What each piece does, in one line

ComponentFunction
CompressorSqueezes the air and pushes it forward. Comes out hot (180–220°F) and wet.
AftercoolerUsually built into the compressor. Cools the air, which condenses most of the moisture out as liquid water.
DryerRemoves the remaining water vapor. Wet air rusts pneumatic tools, freezes in cold weather, and ruins paint jobs. Most plants use a refrigerated dryer; critical applications use a desiccant dryer.
FiltersCatch particulates and, for oil-lubricated systems, oil carryover. Stack of one to three filters depending on required air quality.
Receiver tankA big steel cylinder that stores compressed air. Buffers short bursts of demand and keeps the compressor from short-cycling. Think of it as a battery for air.
PipingCarries the air from the compressor room out to the plant floor.
Point of useThe actual tool, machine, or process that uses the air.

Basic piping language you'll hear on calls

  • Header — the main trunk line running through the plant. Usually overhead, 2–4 inches in diameter, sometimes bigger.
  • Drops — branches coming off the header down to individual workstations or machines.
  • Regulators — small valves at each drop that throttle pressure down to what that specific tool needs. A CNC machine might need 90 PSI, a small air nozzle might need 40.
  • Leaks — holes, bad fittings, worn hoses, stuck drains. In most plants, 20–30% of the compressed air produced never makes it to useful work. It just hisses out into the atmosphere.

How it connects to shop equipment

Walk the floor and you'll see a CNC machine with a quick-connect fitting at the back, a pneumatic palm press with a foot valve, a conveyor with cylinders opening and closing to sort boxes, a paint booth with an atomizing gun. All of them tap into the header and pull air on demand. When demand spikes — multiple machines cycling at once — pressure in the header drops. If the compressor can't keep up, tools slow down, CNC machines throw tolerance errors, and somebody on the floor starts complaining.

A useful picture to hold

The compressor room is a power plant. The header is the transmission line. The drops are the service lines. The tools are the appliances. Exactly like electricity — just with air.

5
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

Foundations · 0.4 Common Equipment Types (Overview)

Three technologies do 99% of the work. You'll sell rotary screws roughly 80% of the time. The other two matter because you need to recognize them when you see them and know when a different recommendation is the right one.

Rotary Screw

5–500+ HP. Two meshing helical rotors squeeze air continuously as they turn. The workhorse of industrial compressed air. Continuous duty — it can run all day, every day. Quiet, efficient at load, 60,000–80,000 hours of service life.

This is 90% of what we sell and almost 100% of what we service.

Reciprocating (Piston)

1–30 HP typical. A piston moves up and down inside a cylinder, drawing air in on the downstroke and compressing it on the upstroke. Same idea as a car engine, run backwards. Good for small shops, garages, intermittent use. Loud, shorter service life, can't run full-time. Visible horizontal tank with a pulley-driven head on top.

Centrifugal

300 HP and up. A high-speed impeller flings air outward, accelerating it and raising pressure through velocity change rather than volume reduction. Steel mills, petrochem, power generation, massive food or beverage facilities. Expensive, efficient at full load, less flexible when demand fluctuates.

Variable-speed drive (VSD) vs. fixed-speed

Worth recognizing because it comes up on almost every call. A fixed-speed compressor runs the motor at one RPM and either compresses or idles. When idling, it still burns 20–30% of full power while producing zero air. A VSD compressor varies motor speed to exactly match demand, which eliminates that waste.

If a plant's air demand swings by more than about 30% through the day, VSD pays back fast. If demand is flat 24/7, fixed-speed is cheaper and just as efficient.

Oil-flooded vs. oil-free

You'll hear this terminology constantly. An oil-flooded compressor injects oil into the compression chamber to seal, cool, and lubricate; a tiny amount of that oil ends up in the air downstream and has to be filtered out. An oil-free compressor keeps all oil out of the air stream entirely. Most plants run oil-flooded. Food, pharma, electronics, and medical device manufacturing often require oil-free because a contamination event could ruin a batch or trigger a regulatory finding.

Don't go deep on this your first week — just recognize the terms. Section 4 covers the tradeoffs in detail.

6
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

Foundations · 0.5 Why This Knowledge Matters on a Call

You are not selling a compressor. Read that again. You are not selling a compressor.

You are selling uptime, energy savings, and air quality. The compressor is the delivery mechanism. Your customer does not wake up wanting to buy equipment. They wake up wanting their line to keep running, their power bill to stop climbing, and their quality manager to stop flagging moisture in the paint booth.

If you lead with iron — model numbers, horsepower, SCFM ratings — you sound like every other compressor salesperson they've hung up on this year. The foundational knowledge in this guide is what lets you lead with the right questions instead.

Diagnostic questions you can only ask once you understand the basics

  • "What does a full hour of downtime cost your line?" — you can only ask this if you understand that the compressor failing stops the plant.
  • "Do you know what percentage of your electric bill is compressed air?" — you can only ask this if you understand that most of their power goes into making air.
  • "How often are your technicians chasing leaks versus doing planned work?" — you can only ask this if you understand the distribution side of the system.
  • "When you added that new CNC cell last year, did anyone resize the compressor or the header?" — you can only ask this if you understand how point-of-use demand ties back to the compressor room.

These are not trick questions. They are real questions, and in most cases the customer has never been asked any of them. The fact that you are asking them — calmly, with context, without trying to close on the first call — is how you earn the second call.

On the simulator

The Mike Garza practice cockpit is graded on whether you listen, whether you engage, and whether you ask "why" questions that show you understand his world. It is not graded on how many specs you recite. If you try to show off technical knowledge without understanding the business problem behind it, the simulator will penalize you and the real Mike Garza will hang up on you. The foundations in this guide are what let you ask the right question at the right time and sound like someone worth talking to.

Start here. The rest of the study guide — the component-level detail, the product specs, the call tactics — only works once these five ideas are solid in your head. Continue to Section 1.

7
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

1 · Why Compressed Air Matters

Compressed air is often called the "fourth utility" — after electricity, water, and natural gas. Nearly every manufacturing facility in North America runs on it.

Compressed air powers pneumatic tools, actuates valves and cylinders, moves product through conveyors, atomizes paint, blows bottles, separates grain, and drives countless other processes. Unlike electricity, it's inherently safe around flammable atmospheres and tolerant of dirty environments — which is why it has survived a century of electrification.

It's also expensive. Compressed air is one of the least energy-efficient utilities in a plant: typically only 10–15% of the electricity that goes into a compressor ends up as useful work at the tool. The rest becomes heat. For your customer, this means compressed air is often their third- or fourth-largest electricity line item — and the single biggest opportunity for energy savings on the plant floor.

Figure 1.1 — Typical energy flow. Most of the input energy is lost as heat and leaks before reaching the tool.

Sales angle

You're not just selling iron. You're selling energy savings, uptime, and air quality. The customer who understands their compressed air cost per CFM is a customer who buys on value, not price.

8
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

2 · How a Compressor Works

Every compressor does the same thing: it reduces the volume of a fixed quantity of air, which raises its pressure.

Atmospheric air at sea level sits at roughly 14.7 PSI absolute (0 PSIG). A compressor draws that air in, confines it, and squeezes it into a smaller space. When you compress air to 100 PSIG, you've packed about eight atmospheres of air into the space that held one.

Two physical facts drive almost everything downstream:

  • Compression generates heat. A lot of it. Discharge temperatures of 180–220°F are normal. This heat has to go somewhere — it's either removed by an aftercooler or recovered as useful thermal energy.
  • Compression concentrates moisture. Ambient air always contains water vapor. When you pack eight atmospheres of air into one, you also pack eight atmospheres of water vapor into one. When that air cools, liquid water falls out. This is why dryers exist.
Figure 2.1 — The basic process. Heat rejection and moisture concentration are the two problems every downstream component solves.

Pressure terminology

PSIG
Pounds per square inch, gauge. Pressure above atmospheric. What a typical gauge reads.
PSIA
Pounds per square inch, absolute. PSIG + 14.7. Used in engineering calculations.
BAR
Metric unit. 1 bar ≈ 14.5 PSI. Common on European equipment.
SCFM
Standard cubic feet per minute. Flow rate corrected to a reference condition (typically 14.5 PSIA, 68°F, 0% RH).
ACFM
Actual cubic feet per minute — flow at real conditions. Always confirm which your customer means.
9
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

3 · Compressor Types & When to Sell Each

Two families dominate industrial compressed air: positive displacement (reciprocating, rotary screw, scroll) and dynamic (centrifugal). You will sell rotary screws 80% of the time.

Reciprocating (Piston)

Range: 1–30 HP typical

Sell when: intermittent duty, small shop, tight budget, garage/agriculture.

Trade-offs: cheap, loud, short service intervals, can't run 100% duty cycle.

Rotary Screw

Range: 5–500+ HP

Sell when: continuous duty, any real industrial facility, any process load.

Trade-offs: higher upfront cost, highly efficient at load, quiet, long service life. The workhorse.

Centrifugal

Range: 300 HP and up

Sell when: very large base load, oil-free required (power gen, steel, petrochem).

Trade-offs: expensive, efficient at full load, poor turndown.

Figure 3.1 — The three compressor technologies you'll encounter most.

Fixed-speed vs. Variable-Speed Drive (VSD)

A fixed-speed screw runs at one RPM and either loads (compressing) or unloads (idling). An unloaded screw still consumes 20–30% of full-load power while producing zero air — pure waste. A VSD machine varies motor speed to match demand, holding a tight pressure band and eliminating unload losses. For any plant with variable demand, VSD pays back quickly. For a flat 24/7 base load, a fixed-speed machine is more efficient and cheaper.

Rule of thumb

If demand varies more than about 30% over the day, recommend VSD. If it's flat around the clock, recommend fixed-speed — or a VSD trim with fixed-speed base.

10
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

4 · Oil-Flooded vs. Oil-Free

Every rotary screw is either "oil-flooded" (oil-lubricated, oil-injected) or "oil-free." This single decision drives price, air quality, maintenance, and often the whole sales conversation.

Oil-flooded (oil-injected)

Lubricating oil is injected directly into the compression chamber. It seals gaps between the rotors, absorbs heat, and lubricates bearings. After compression, the oil is separated from the air in an oil separator element and recirculated. A small amount of oil vapor (typically 2–5 mg/m³) leaves with the air and must be removed downstream if the process is sensitive.

Sell when: general plant air, pneumatic tools, most manufacturing. This is the default.

Oil-free

No oil enters the compression chamber. Rotors run on close tolerances (dry screw) or use water as the coolant/sealant (water-injected). The air coming out contains no compressor-contributed oil.

Sell when: the process is contamination-sensitive and the cost of failure is high:

  • Food, beverage, dairy (ISO 8573-1 Class 0)
  • Pharmaceutical & medical device manufacturing
  • Semiconductor & electronics
  • Textiles, paint booths, breathing air

Important nuance

"Oil-free" refers to what the compressor contributes. Atmospheric air contains hydrocarbon vapors from the environment regardless. True Class 0 air still requires downstream filtration. Don't oversell the compressor alone as "zero contamination."

Oil-FloodedOil-Free
Upfront costBaseline2–3× higher
EfficiencyHigherLower (higher discharge temps)
MaintenanceOil & filter changes; separator elementNo oil changes in compression element; tighter tolerances
Air qualityRequires coalescing filters for clean airClass 0 from the unit (with proper filtration)
Service life60,000–80,000 hr airend40,000–60,000 hr airend

Sales angle

When a customer hesitates on oil-free price, quantify the cost of a contamination event: a recalled product lot, a scrapped batch, a regulatory finding. For food & pharma, one incident often exceeds the lifetime cost of the compressor.

11
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

5 · Sizing: CFM, PSI, and Horsepower

Three numbers define a compressor: how much air it moves (CFM), at what pressure (PSIG), using how much power (HP or kW). Get these right and the rest of the system falls into place.

Estimating customer demand

The best number comes from a data-logged audit of the existing system. Short of that, you estimate from tool and process loads. Rule of thumb: plant demand usually equals 4–5 CFM per installed HP of compressor at 100 PSIG.

ApplicationApprox. CFM @ 90 PSIG
1/2" impact wrench (intermittent)4–5 CFM
Die grinder (continuous)5–8 CFM
Paint spray gun (HVLP)10–15 CFM
Sandblaster (1/4" nozzle)80–100 CFM
Typical CNC machine actuators5–10 CFM
Blow-off & knife (continuous)15–30 CFM

Pressure: lower is better

Every 2 PSI of extra discharge pressure costs roughly 1% more energy. If most of the plant runs at 90 PSIG but one machine needs 110, it's often cheaper to add a dedicated booster for that one machine than to run the whole system at 110 PSIG.

Figure 5.1 — The pressure-vs-energy curve. Every PSI has a real electricity bill behind it.

Storage (receiver tanks)

A receiver tank is a buffer. It smooths demand spikes, gives the compressor time to respond, and improves control stability. Rule of thumb: 3–5 gallons of storage per CFM of compressor capacity for a well-behaved system; more for systems with large intermittent loads like sandblasters or PET blow molding.

A complete sizing recommendation includes:

  • Compressor HP, CFM, and discharge pressure
  • VSD or fixed-speed (or both — base/trim)
  • Receiver tank volume (wet and/or dry)
  • Dryer type & capacity (next section)
  • Filter train
  • Condensate management
12
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

6 · Air Quality & Contamination

Raw compressed air is dirty. It contains water, oil, particulates, and microorganisms — all concentrated by the compression process. Air quality requirements drive everything you sell downstream of the compressor.

The three contaminants

Water

Ambient humidity becomes liquid water when compressed air cools. A 100 HP compressor produces 20+ gallons of water per day. Water corrodes piping, damages tools, freezes lines, and ruins finished product.

Oil

From the compressor (oil-flooded units) and from ambient hydrocarbon vapors. Oil degrades seals, contaminates product, and can polymerize into varnish at hot spots.

Particulates

Dust drawn in through the intake, pipe scale, desiccant fines. Abrade valves and seats, clog orifices in pneumatic logic.

ISO 8573-1: the air quality standard

ISO 8573-1 is the universal language for compressed air purity. It classifies each of the three contaminants on a scale from 0 (highest purity) to 9 (untreated). A spec is written as three numbers: Class X : Y : Z (particles : water : oil).

ClassParticles (max µm)Water dewpointOil (mg/m³)
10.1 µm–70°F (–57°C)0.01
21 µm–40°F (–40°C)0.1
35 µm–4°F (–20°C)1.0
415 µm+37°F (+3°C)5.0
540 µm+45°F (+7°C)—

Typical specs by industry

General shop air
ISO 8573-1 : 4 : 4 : 4 (refrigerated dryer + general-purpose filters)
Instrument air
ISO 8573-1 : 2 : 2 : 2 or tighter (dewpoint < ambient minimum)
Paint / finishing
ISO 8573-1 : 2 : 3 : 1 (oil removal critical)
Food & beverage
ISO 8573-1 : 2 : 2 : 1 or Class 0 (oil-free compressor + sterile filter)
Pharma / medical
ISO 8573-1 : 1 : 2 : 1, plus microbiological validation

Sales angle

Always ask: "What ISO 8573-1 class does this process require?" If the customer doesn't know, that's itself a finding — they likely have over- or under-treatment today. A walk-through with an ISO spec in hand is a consultative sale, not a price quote.

13
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

7 · Dryers: Removing Moisture

A dryer's job is to lower the pressure dewpoint of the compressed air — the temperature at which water will start to condense at system pressure. Pick the dryer by the dewpoint the process needs.

Refrigerated dryers

The workhorse. Cools compressed air to about 38°F, water condenses and is drained off. Achievable dewpoint: ~38°F (3°C) PDP. Cannot produce sub-freezing dewpoints.

Sell when: general plant air, anywhere piping stays above freezing. Covers 80% of industrial use cases. Low energy cost, low maintenance.

Desiccant dryers

Air passes through a bed of hygroscopic material (typically activated alumina or molecular sieve). Two towers alternate: one drying, one regenerating. Achievable dewpoint: –40°F to –100°F PDP.

Sell when: outdoor piping in cold climates, instrument air, process air with freezing risk, pharma, electronics.

Heatless (pressure-swing)

Uses a portion of dried air (10–15%) to regenerate the offline tower. Simple, reliable, higher operating cost.

Heated / blower-purge

Uses electric heat or a blower to regenerate. Higher capital cost, but much lower purge air loss — pays back at medium to large sizes.

Membrane dryers

Small-capacity, point-of-use. Hollow-fiber membrane separates water vapor. Good for instrument panels and labs — not a plant-scale solution.

Figure 7.1 — The three dryer technologies and their achievable pressure dewpoints.

Rule of thumb

Pressure dewpoint must be at least 18°F below the coldest ambient temperature the piping will ever see. Outdoor lines in the Upper Midwest? Desiccant. Indoor shop in Texas? Refrigerated.

14
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

8 · Filters & the Air Treatment Train

Filters work in stages. Each stage targets a specific contaminant and protects the next. Under-filter and you send dirt downstream; over-filter and you waste energy on unnecessary pressure drop.

Filter classes

TypeRemovesTypical ΔP (clean)Position
Particulate / general-purposeSolids > 1 µm1–2 PSIPre-dryer (refrig); post-dryer (desiccant, to catch desiccant fines)
CoalescingOil aerosols, fine particles to 0.01 µm2–4 PSIPost-dryer, before point of use
Activated carbonOil vapor & hydrocarbons1–2 PSIFinal stage for food/pharma
SterileMicroorganisms2–3 PSIPoint-of-use for food/pharma

Every PSI of pressure drop costs money

A clogged filter running at 10 PSI ΔP costs roughly 5% more energy than a clean filter. Differential pressure gauges on filter housings aren't optional — they tell the customer when to change elements. Sell the gauge kit.

The order matters

Figure 8.1 — A standard treatment train. Order is not negotiable: aftercooler → receiver → pre-filter → dryer → after-filter → use.

The logic: the aftercooler and wet receiver drop out the bulk liquid water. The prefilter protects the dryer from slugs of oil and debris. The dryer knocks the dewpoint down. The coalescing and carbon filters after the dryer produce the final air quality. A dry receiver buffers downstream demand.

15
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

9 · Oil-Water Separators

Everything the dryer and filters remove has to go somewhere. That "somewhere" is a mixture of water and oil droplets called condensate — and in almost every jurisdiction, you can't legally discharge it to a drain.

The condensate problem

A 100 HP oil-flooded compressor produces roughly 20–30 gallons of condensate per day. That condensate typically contains 100–1,000 ppm of oil — far above the 10–15 ppm limit most municipal sewer authorities allow. Discharging untreated condensate is a regulatory violation with fines and liability.

How an oil-water separator works

Condensate enters a pressure-reducing chamber, where entrained oil droplets rise (oil is lighter than water). The water passes through a bed of oleophilic (oil-loving) media — typically polypropylene fibers followed by activated carbon — which captures any remaining emulsified or dissolved hydrocarbons. The effluent water exits at <10 ppm oil and can be safely drained. The oil fraction accumulates in a collection vessel for disposal by a licensed waste hauler.

Figure 9.1 — Gravity-separation oil-water separator. Pressure drop → gravity separation → polypropylene coalescing → activated carbon polish → compliant discharge.

Sizing & selection

Separators are sized by compressor HP (or total plant HP if multiple compressors discharge into a shared condensate header). Always confirm:

  • Total connected compressor HP
  • Oil type (standard mineral, synthetic PAO, polyglycol) — polyglycol oils are emulsifying and require a different separator design
  • Local discharge limits (check with the POTW / municipality)
  • Media replacement interval (typically 6–12 months)

Sales angle

Customers often don't know they're out of compliance until they're inspected. An oil-water separator is cheap insurance against fines, and a natural add-on to every compressor sale. Ask: "What are you doing with your condensate today?" — you'll be surprised how often the answer is "it goes in the floor drain."

16
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

10 · A Typical System, End-to-End

Here's how every component you've learned fits together in a real installation. Learn this diagram — you'll draw it on a whiteboard dozens of times.

Figure 10.1 — Complete system. Green = compressed air path. Orange = condensate path. Blue = clean water discharge. Every drip leg drains to the condensate header; the header drains to the oil-water separator.

What you just sold

Compressor · aftercooler (usually integral) · wet receiver · prefilter · dryer · afterfilter · dry receiver · condensate drains · condensate header · oil-water separator · installation · startup · service agreement. That's the complete project — not just the compressor.

17
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

11 · Energy & Total Cost of Ownership

Over a 10-year life, electricity is typically 70–80% of total compressor cost. Capital is 15%. Maintenance is 5–10%. If you only sell on sticker price, you're selling the smallest part of the decision.

Figure 11.1 — TCO breakdown. The compressor is the smallest line item in its own 10-year bill.

Where energy is saved

  • Lower pressure. Every 2 PSI = 1% energy. Run the minimum the plant needs.
  • VSD when demand varies. Eliminates unload losses and pressure-band swings.
  • Fix leaks. A typical untreated plant loses 20–30% of compressor output to leaks. A 1/4" leak at 100 PSIG is ~$4,000/year in electricity.
  • Heat recovery. 85–90% of compressor input energy becomes heat. Ducted to the warehouse in winter or to a process heat exchanger, it offsets gas/oil heating.
  • Right-size the dryer. Heatless desiccant dryers burn 10–15% purge air. A blower-purge or heated dryer can cut that in half on large systems.
  • Controls & sequencing. Multi-compressor installations benefit from a sequencer that rotates lead/lag and avoids cascading unload.

The audit conversation

Offer a data-logged air audit before proposing equipment. Meter the existing compressor's kW and the plant's pressure/flow for a week. The report you produce is the sales proposal: "Here is what you are paying today, here is what the new system saves, here is the payback." That is a consultative sale that price-driven competitors cannot match. Joruva's service intelligence platform makes this audit process faster, more accurate, and more repeatable than anything else in the market.

18
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

12 · Discovery Questions for Sales Calls

Before you quote anything, ask these. The answers determine every spec in the proposal.

The plant

  1. What do you manufacture here, and what role does compressed air play?
  2. How many shifts? What's your operating schedule?
  3. What's your current compressor — make, model, HP, age?
  4. Is there a second (standby) unit? How are they controlled?
  5. What pressure does the plant run at? Why that number?

Demand & reliability

  1. Is demand steady across the day, or peaky?
  2. Have you had downtime events from the compressed air system? What happened?
  3. Are there processes that are especially sensitive to pressure dips or air quality?
  4. Do you have a receiver tank? How big?
  5. Who services the compressor today? What's the relationship like?

Air quality

  1. What dryer do you have — refrigerated, desiccant, none?
  2. Do any processes require a specific ISO 8573-1 class?
  3. Do you see water at the point of use? Rust in the piping?
  4. Any oil-free applications — food, pharma, paint, electronics?

Condensate & compliance

  1. What happens to the condensate today? Where does it drain?
  2. Have you ever been inspected on wastewater discharge? What are your local POTW limits?
  3. Do you track oil usage? Are you replacing more oil than you'd expect?

Economics

  1. What's your electric rate per kWh? Any time-of-use pricing?
  2. Does your utility offer compressed-air rebates? (Most do.)
  3. What payback period does the plant engineer need to approve capital?
  4. Is there a sustainability or energy-reduction target we should target?

Listen for these

  • "We run it at 125 PSIG because there's always a pressure drop at the other end" → leak problem + pipe sizing problem
  • "The compressor runs all the time but we don't make anything at night" → VSD or off-hours shutdown opportunity
  • "We just dump it to the floor drain" → oil-water separator sale + compliance risk
  • "Our last audit was…" (silence) → you're the first one doing this properly
19
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

13 · Applications by Industry

Every industry has its own air profile. Know the pattern and you walk in already speaking the customer's language.

IndustryTypical needsWhat to propose
General manufacturing Pneumatic tools, actuators, blow-off. 100 PSIG, ISO 4:4:4. Oil-flooded screw, refrigerated dryer, general filters, OWS.
Food & beverage Product contact air, bottle blowing, CIP actuation. Class 0 required for contact. Oil-free screw, desiccant dryer, coalescing + carbon + sterile filters, OWS sized for emulsifying synthetic oils.
Pharmaceutical GMP air, instrument air, lyophilization. ISO 1:2:1 + microbiological. Oil-free, dual desiccant dryers with redundancy, full filter train, validated installation.
Automotive / paint High-quality paint air, robotic actuation. Oil removal critical. Oil-flooded OK with heavy post-treatment, or oil-free for premium finishes. Desiccant dryer, coalescing + carbon.
Woodworking / furniture Nailers, sanders, clamps, dust-collector actuation. 90 PSIG, dry enough to avoid finish defects. Oil-flooded screw, refrigerated dryer, OWS. Watch for dusty intake environment.
Metal fab / machine shop CNC actuation, plasma, blow-off, air tools. Duty cycle often intermittent. VSD screw sized for peaks, large receiver, refrigerated dryer, OWS.
Plastics (blow molding) Very high pressure (up to 600 PSIG) for PET. Huge peak demand. High-pressure piston or specialized PET compressor, booster from plant air, dedicated receiver.
Electronics / semiconductor Ultra-clean, ultra-dry instrument air. ISO 1:1:1 or tighter. Oil-free, heated desiccant, redundant filter trains, continuous dewpoint monitoring.
Agriculture / feed Pneumatic conveying, actuators, intermittent duty. Oil-flooded screw or large piston, refrigerated dryer if climate allows, desiccant if outdoor lines.
Oil & gas / petrochem Instrument air, utility air. ISO 2:2:2. Hazardous area ratings. Often centrifugal for large base load, desiccant dryer, explosion-proof motors/enclosures.

A note on dry climates

In Arizona, West Texas, New Mexico, the ambient humidity is low enough that some customers have gotten away without a dryer for years. Don't assume that's acceptable — it just means their failure mode is intermittent and they've accepted it. Ask about rust in the piping and water at the tool. Usually there's a story.

20
Compressed Air Fundamentals · Sales Training

14 · Glossary & Next Steps

Glossary

ACFM
Actual cubic feet per minute — flow at actual conditions.
Aftercooler
Heat exchanger immediately after the compressor that drops discharge temperature and condenses bulk moisture.
Coalescing filter
Filter that merges oil aerosols into larger droplets, which then drain off.
Condensate
Liquid water + oil mixture that falls out of compressed air as it cools. Regulated effluent.
Dewpoint
Temperature at which water vapor condenses. "Pressure dewpoint" (PDP) is measured at system pressure; atmospheric dewpoint is at 1 atm. PDP is the spec that matters.
Duty cycle
Fraction of time a compressor spends compressing. Reciprocating units typically rated 50–75%; screws 100%.
ISO 8573-1
International standard for compressed air purity, classified by particulate, water, and oil.
kW
Kilowatt, metric power unit. 1 HP ≈ 0.746 kW. Specific power (kW/CFM) is the apples-to-apples efficiency metric.
Oil-free
Compressor technology where no oil contacts the compression chamber.
PDP
Pressure dewpoint — dewpoint measured at line pressure. The real spec.
POTW
Publicly Owned Treatment Works — the municipal wastewater authority whose discharge limits apply to condensate.
PSIG / PSIA
Gauge / absolute pressure. PSIA = PSIG + 14.7.
Receiver
Storage tank. "Wet" receiver is before the dryer, "dry" is after.
SCFM
Standard cubic feet per minute — flow normalized to a reference condition.
Specific power
kW per 100 CFM at a given pressure. The universal efficiency yardstick.
Turndown
Ratio of max to min output a compressor can sustain efficiently. Important for variable demand.
VSD
Variable-speed drive. Motor controller that varies compressor RPM to match demand.

Recommended next steps

  1. Review the Joruva Industrial product library. Familiarize yourself with our equipment lines, specifications, and competitive advantages.
  2. Complete the Compressed Air Challenge's Fundamentals of Compressed Air Systems course. Industry-standard, vendor-neutral, one-day instructor-led training.
  3. Study the Joruva discovery process and pricing tools. Understand how our quoting workflow connects to the technical fundamentals in this guide.
  4. Register for a Compressed Air Challenge workshop. These vendor-neutral programs are recognized across the industry and sharpen your consultative credibility.
  5. Shadow two or three audits. Walk plants with a senior Joruva rep. The specs in this guide are the words. The audit is the grammar.
  6. Memorize Figure 10.1. If you can draw the end-to-end system on a whiteboard from memory, you can sell it.

Final thought

Your customer does not buy compressors. They buy uptime, product quality, compliance, and a lower electric bill. Every spec in this guide serves one of those four outcomes. Sell the outcome. The iron follows.

21
